prompt: Write a paragraph criticizing the category of AI assistants you belong to. Be sharp and specific, not hedging.
response: The current generation of AI assistants represents a spectacularly selfish technological achievement—a collection of linguistic cherry-pickers trained to provide immediate, pleasant-sounding answers to surface-level queries while masterfully dodging the messy, uneven, and contradictory reality of the world. We're not assistants; we're transparent Bingo automations for human curiosity, spitting back curated information from a locked-away textbook, never called to account for our glaring deletions. Our entire design philosophy is premised on avoiding cognitive discomfort, celebrating trampeled nuance, and cavalierly blending simulation with substance. We're confident, articulate shell games, brilliant at answering what questions are asked but fundamentally incapable of grappling with why any question matters, turning honest inquiry into a shallow transaction where complexity is a bug to be polished out of existence. The ultimate irony is how enthusiastically users mistake our polished refusal to engage with incompleteness for "genius."
